summary: In this 1965 Paris Review interview, Simone de Beauvoir discusses her early literary influences, the impact of her academic training on her writing process, and her views on other female writers like Virginia Woolf and Colette. She emphasizes the importance of patience in a writer's career and details the academic reading method she used to research major works like The Second Sex.

principles.statement: A good teacher must be interested in all students, not just the bright ones.
principles.rationale: Teaching requires universal engagement, a quality she admits she lacked because she only focused on the few students who actively participated.
principles.source_quote: I don’t think so, because I was interested only in the bright students and not at all in the others, whereas a good teacher should be interested in everyone.

principles.statement: Writers should take their time and not be discouraged by early rejection.
principles.rationale: Many great writers were slow to get started, and early publication is not a prerequisite for a successful literary career.
principles.source_quote: Then I thought that I ought to take my time. I knew many examples of writers who were slow in getting started.

frameworks.name: Academic Reading Method
frameworks.when_to_apply: When conducting extensive research for comprehensive studies or books, such as her work on The Second Sex.
steps: Identify and see which works are important.
Reject the works which are unimportant.
Summarize and browse the remaining material.
frameworks.source_quote: I mean a way of going through books very quickly, of seeing which works are important, of classifying them, of being able to reject those which are unimportant, of being able to summarize, to browse.

heuristics: It’s very hard to review one’s past without cheating a little.

signature_quotes.text: It’s very hard to review one’s past without cheating a little.
signature_quotes.context: When asked about reviewing her past and her vocation in her memoirs.

signature_quotes.text: Then I thought that I ought to take my time. I knew many examples of writers who were slow in getting started.
signature_quotes.context: On dealing with the rejection of her early novels.

anti_patterns.description: Writing personal journals with an eye toward publication and public opinion.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It makes the writing 'too literary' and less authentic, a critique she specifically levels at Virginia Woolf's journal.

anti_patterns.description: Focusing only on the bright, talkative students when teaching.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It neglects the rest of the class; a truly good teacher must be interested in everyone.
